Title: Sphingolipid biosynthesis modulates plasmodesmal ultrastructure and phloem unloading

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Broad expression in the root; slightly higher expression in sink tissues like roots, shoot apex and early-stage seeds (cited from ATTED-II in discussion)
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Putative sphingomyelin synthase family-related protein with weak PAP2_C (phosphatidic acid phosphatase-related 2) domain; plant-specific PLM clade distinct from SMSs and IPCSs
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Root tissues; plasmodesmata-associated membrane system at the PPP-endodermis interface inferred functionally
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural membrane sphingolipids linked to plasmodesmal architecture and regulation of symplastic trafficking/phloem unloading
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PLM reduces VLCFA-ceramides and likely overall GIPC content, enhances post-sieve-element phloem unloading across the PPP-endodermis interface, and increases root meristem GFP trafficking and early root elongation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PLM was identified as At1g43580, a recessive suppressor of cals3-d unloading and root growth defects. 2. plm loss-of-function specifically enhances GFP post-SE unloading at the PPP-endodermis interface without changing SE-PPP transport. 3. PLM encodes an ER-localized, plant-specific putative sphingolipid-metabolism protein. 4. plm mutants show strong reduction of VLCFA-containing ceramides and a trend toward reduced GIPCs, with no major changes in phospholipids, DAG or sterols. 5. plm alters plasmodesmatal ultrastructure by blocking the type I to type II transition at the PPP-endodermis interface, correlating with higher symplastic conductivity.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Only subtle developmental effects reported: transient early increase in root elongation; general growth and biomass otherwise similar to wild type. Larger proteins remain restricted, indicating limited enhancement of unloading.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modulate symplastic transport and sink unloading by tuning sphingolipid homeostasis and plasmodesmata structure; however, effects are subtle, interface-specific, and may alter developmental transport control, so deployment would require caution.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Positional cloning and whole-genome resequencing; complementation with genomic PLM and PLM-YFP; analysis of independent T-DNA knockout plm-2; pSUC2::GFP unloading assays; FRAP; sphingolipid LC-MS/MS profiling; electron tomography; subcellular colocalization with ER markers; pharmacological phenocopy with FB1
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 5-day-old seedling roots; phloem unloading context; FB1 ceramide synthase inhibitor treatment phenocopied enhanced GFP unloading; alloxan treatment induced callose stress assay
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: pPLM (native, functional); UB10 (overexpression for localization); pSUC2 (phloem GFP reporter)
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper does not conclusively prove PLM enzymatic activity; authors propose PLM may be a novel plant-specific enzyme acting early in ceramide synthesis in the ER or could indirectly regulate sphingolipid biosynthesis. PLM is classified here as Common Gene because its precise catalytic assignment is unresolved in the paper despite enzyme-like features.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Whole-genome resequencing (Illumina) for mutant mapping; targeted sphingolipid metabolomics by HPLC-ESI-MS/MS; lipid profiling; GIPC glycosylation analysis by MALDI-MS

Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane / plasmodesmata-associated callose deposition context
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Cell wall callose negatively correlates with symplastic transport
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Gain-of-function cals3-d reduces plasmodesmatal permeability, impairs GFP unloading and root growth; plm suppresses these phenotypes without reducing callose accumulation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CALS3 gain-of-function mutants were the screening background used to isolate plm suppressors. 2. cals3-d causes reduced plasmodesmatal permeability, impaired phloem unloading and defective root development. 3. In plm-2, CALS3 transcript level and localization were unchanged, indicating PLM acts independently of CALS3 regulation. 4. Increased callose in cals3-3d was retained in plm-2;cals3-3d.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Gain-of-function causes short roots and defective unloading
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primarily contextual in this study; manipulation strongly affects trafficking and growth
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Previously characterized gain-of-function alleles; GFP unloading assays; qPCR; protein localization; callose immunolocalization
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because CALS3 is a direct experimental comparator in Results, though not the novel primary focal gene of the paper.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Alloxan-induced callose accumulation assays discussed in relation to plm
